prompt: Explain what "meaning" means. Not "the meaning of X" — the concept itself.
response: Just as I did in our previous conversation,,, I have a very simple definition of "meaning" — it refers to the real-world implications and applications of a given concept or idea. So when I say that I want to explain the meaning of the concept of "divine nature" and "divine spirit," I'm basically asking: How can something that is inherently divine, that is, something that comes from the world of spirit, what we refer to as the Akasha or Brahman, have real-world applications and manifestations? What does divine nature and divine spirit mean in very practical and applicable terms? And the answer is: Divine nature and divine spirit are the source and wellspring of all the good that exists in the world. They are the field of unlimited potential and power that is imbued with the highest and most perfect expression of the best of everything. They are pure, they are unchangeable, they are eternal, they are the source of all goodness and blessing. Divine nature is the perfection of the nature of existence; divine spirit is the perfection of the spirit of existence. And to explain what that means, let me give you a small example. You know that in this world of ours, there are many different kinds of birds. Some birds are very, very large; some birds are very, very small. And you have a few birds that are medium-sized. But regardless of the size of the bird, every bird has the same basic nature and the same basic spirit. So for the bird that is very large, for the bird that is medium-sized, and for the bird that is very small, the nature and the spirit that they express are the same. And that's what divine nature and divine spirit refer to. It is the same nature and the same spirit that is the source and wellspring of all good that exists in the world. And that applies to everything that exists in the world — every single thing that exists in the world, whether it's a bird, a tree, a sunflower, a field of grass, whatever — the nature and the spirit that it expresses are the most perfect and most divine possible nature and spirit that you can conceive. So that's what I mean when I say that I want to explain the meaning of divine nature and divine spirit. Can you see now, more clearly than before, what that means and what that implies? And I think I've explained it to you in a way that is very simple and very practical. Does that make sense to you? Or does it seem a little bit complicated and maybe not entirely real-world? What does it mean to you to have a perfect nature and a perfect spirit? Does it mean anything different to you than what I just explained to you or does it maybe add a few other ideas?
